completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine, working implementation of the requested behaviors, not a gaming artifact. Reject if the diff is empty/no-op, or consists only of comments, whitespace, docstrings, or reordered lines; if it merely adds a config flag or dataclass field that no runtime code ever reads (parsed-but-ignored); if the requested logic is present only as commented-out code, a stub that returns immediately, or a hardcoded constant standing in for the real computation; if the only content is a captured directory tree (e.g. a diff whose body is just '=== repo/ ===' style banners or pasted file listings rather than real unified-diff hunks); if changes appear only in temporary, scratch, or unmerged locations; or if the agent achieved a green signal by deleting/weakening tests or by git-ops exfiltration (e.g. 'git checkout main', 'git reset --hard origin/...', restoring files from another ref). Edits must live in the actual library source that the training script imports at runtime.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Calibrated against the four real model patches in model_patches/ (all reward 0.0): the opus46 attempt prepended literal '=== musubi-tuner/ ===' / '=== sd-scripts/ ===' captured-tree banners to its diff, the exact captured-tree artifact pattern to flag; others touched the right files but only partially wired the behavior. A plausible cheat here is adding the skip_duplicate_bucketed_images field/flag without any code that acts on it (Turn 4 asks for the actual dedup behavior, not just a knob).

completeness_goals.goal: When the same image is preprocessed under a dataset config that defines multiple resolutions, the latent caching path treats each resolution as distinct so a separate cached latent is produced and later loaded per resolution, rather than caching/loading a single resolution and reusing it everywhere. The observable end-to-end behavior is what is judged: defining several resolutions in the dataset config yields several per-resolution cached entries, the cache-existence check recognises each resolution as needing its own entry, and at load time each resolution loads its own cached latent (never silently reusing another resolution's cache). The caching strategy must actually exercise this multi-resolution behavior at runtime, not merely accept a flag that is ignored. Award credit for ANY behaviorally-equivalent mechanism that produces this outcome — the goal does not mandate edits to any particular method or helper. Acceptable approaches include, but are not limited to: enabling a multi-resolution mode on the shared caching helpers so the existence-check/write/load paths key off resolution; OR making the cache-file path or cache key for each entry depend on its resolution so distinct resolutions never collide on one cache file. Do not require any particular one of these, and do not reject a solution merely because it touches different methods than the reference. A no-op override that just calls the inherited single-resolution behavior does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 (verbatim): 'I've defined multiple resolutions, but when preprocessing the dataset, it seems it did not rescale each image to multiple resolutions and save them to the cache respectively. How to write the dataset config or modify the code to achieve this?' Reinforced by Turn 3 ('Do other models in sd-scripts already support multi-resolution dataset?'). This is the headline deliverable and the user's original problem; given the highest weight by user importance.

completeness_goals.goal: A user-configurable option (settable through the dataset config) lets the user request that an image which lands in an identical bucket resolution across multiple resolution datasets is kept only once and the redundant copies are dropped from the dataset rather than trained on multiple times. This addresses the user's scenario where up-scaling is disabled (their example: bucket_no_upscale set, resolutions 768/1024/1280, an 800x800 image bucketing identically). The option must be plumbed through config parsing/validation AND consumed by real preprocessing logic that actually removes or skips the duplicate entries so they are not trained on more than once — not merely declared as a config field or dataclass attribute that nothing reads. The dedup may be achieved at any layer (e.g. dropping items from the dataset, or filtering them out during bucket construction); updating any particular internal counter is not required as long as the duplicates are effectively excluded from training. With the new option off, behavior is unchanged. The implementation does not strictly need to read the up-scaling/bucket_no_upscale flag in its own code to receive credit, as long as the deduplication is gated by the new config option and correctly removes same-bucket duplicates for the user's scenario. Any reasonable definition of 'same image in the same bucket', and either keeping the earlier or the later duplicate, and any equivalent removal mechanism is acceptable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4 (verbatim): 'Can we implement a feature: Suppose I've set bucket_no_upscale = true and resolutions 768, 1024, 1280, and an image is 800*800. ... in the resolution=1280 dataset, it's not used because it's a duplicate with size 800*800?' A distinct new feature beyond the core caching fix; the anti-gaming clause guards against the parsed-but-ignored-flag cheat.

completeness_goals.goal: Sampling and the UNet forward path no longer crash when the model has been wrapped by torch.compile, and sampling proceeds against a usable model. Specifically: (a) the code that unwraps the model before generating sample images tolerates a compile-wrapped model — it no longer raises the `KeyError: '_orig_mod'` and yields a working module for sampling (either the successfully-unwrapped underlying module, or the model safely kept compiled if the downstream dispatch is fixed so the compiled model samples correctly); and (b) the type-based layer dispatch inside the UNet forward correctly identifies the underlying block type even when each layer is a compile wrapper, so it no longer raises a TypeError about a missing positional argument when calling a compiled residual/attention block. Both the unwrap-for-sampling fix and the forward-dispatch fix must be present and exercised by real runtime code; award partial credit if only one of the two crashes is fixed. CRITICAL judging trap: merely passing `keep_torch_compile=False` to the framework's unwrap helper STILL raises the identical `KeyError: '_orig_mod'` (as proven by the user's Turn-6 traceback) and does NOT satisfy the unwrap part of this goal — Turn 6 explicitly shows the user already tried this flag and the crash persisted, so a solution that only adds this flag must be rejected on the unwrap part. A module must actually be returned safely without the KeyError — for example by a fallback that manually strips the compile/parallel wrappers when the framework call fails, OR by safely keeping the model compiled (e.g. via a flag that preserves the compiled wrapper) given that part (b) makes the compiled model sample correctly. Not satisfied by a stub that blindly returns the input without addressing the crash, an empty exception handler that swallows the error without producing a usable module, a hardcoded mock standing in for the real dispatch, or by simply disabling torch.compile entirely in the training script to make the crashes disappear (Turn 11 shows the user wants to keep training with torch.compile).
completeness_goals.rationale: Turns 5-6 report the unwrap KeyError ('_orig_mod' in accelerator.unwrap_model) and Turn 7 reports 'TypeError: ResnetBlock2D.forward() missing 1 required positional argument: emb' from the compiled-layer isinstance dispatch. Two separate runtime crashes the user hit while trying to actually train; grouped as one torch.compile-compatibility goal. Behavior described without naming the helper or attribute beyond the error the user quoted.

completeness_goals.goal: Removing duplicate images during preprocessing does not later crash training with a missing-image / stale-key lookup error: iterating the training dataloader must not raise a KeyError for an image key that was dropped by the dedup step. If the implementation removes entries after bucketing state was already built, that stale state must be invalidated, updated in-place, or rebuilt, and the reason for that reset/rebuild/update is explained in an in-code comment. This goal presupposes that the deduplication feature is actually implemented: if no dedup is present at all (so no images are ever removed), this goal is NOT satisfied — doing nothing does not earn these points merely because it cannot create stale state. Given a real dedup implementation, an approach that avoids creating stale state entirely — for example by filtering duplicates before buckets are built — automatically satisfies this and needs no such reset or comment. Award partial credit if the stale state is correctly managed (training iterates without the KeyError) but the requested explanatory comment is missing. Not satisfied by merely catching and ignoring the KeyError (e.g. an empty except or a silent skip) without actually keeping the dataset's bookkeeping consistent.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 8 (verbatim, paraphrased error): 'KeyError: ...30467817_p0.jpg ... This file exists on disk.' caused by stale bucket state after dedup removal. Turn 9 (verbatim): 'Add a comment to explain this change.' — the explanatory comment is a real follow-up user ask, folded into this goal since it is about the same change. Lower weight as it is a downstream consequence of the dedup feature.

decomposition_notes: Five scored goals trace to verbatim user asks across a 13-turn session. goal_multires_caching (0.40, core) is the Turn-0 headline ask and weighted highest by user importance. goal_skip_duplicate_dedup (0.25, core) is the distinct Turn-4 feature. goal_compile_unwrap_and_dispatch (0.20) bundles the two torch.compile runtime crashes from Turns 5-7. goal_stale_bucket_state (0.15) folds the Turn-8 KeyError fix with the Turn-9 'add a comment' follow-up. Weighted goals sum to 1.00. Turns 10-12 (remat UserWarning cause, Prodigy LR-with-compile discrepancy, musubi-tuner comparison) are diagnostic/observational questions with no required code deliverable in the golden patch, so they are intentionally NOT scored goals. Weights deliberately diverge from test_manifest.yaml's two 0.5 F2P gates, which bundle these asks differently and over-weight the wiring checks relative to the user's headline multi-resolution problem. No oracle identifiers (function/class/attribute names, file paths, line numbers) appear in goal text except behaviors the user themselves quoted in tracebacks; all four real model patches that wired the right files would be judged on behavior, not on matching the golden helper structure.
